coredata.dc:title: Toxicity reduction of graphene quantum dots by photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lijuan
Wang, Lingzhi
Chen, Gengrui
Li, Wenxi
Ge, Hongzhou
Tang, Fuqiang
Wen, Yating
Feng, Yanxian
Wu, Haoyi
coredata.dc:description: The widespread use and uncontrolled diffusion of nano-carbon materials are bringing threats to human health because the cytotoxicity of such materials increases over certain in-vivo concentrations. In the present study, the cytotoxicity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was reduced after photocatalysis with TiO2 and ultraviolet light. The FTIR and TEM analysis suggested that the hydrophilic functional groups of GQDs were removed, causing the aggregation of the nanoparticles and the reduction of water-diffused concentration. The treatment is valuable for the disposal of nano-carbon materials in the wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Cytotoxicity
Biosafety

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic diagram of synthesizing GQDs; (b) AFM image of GQDs.
TEM of the GQDs (a) before and after (b) photocatalytic treatment.
The FTIR of the GQDs (a) before and (b) after photocatalytic treatment.

(a) Excitation and emission spectra of GQDs before (solid line) and after (dash line) photocatalytic treatment (30 min), (b) The change of the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of GQDs in UV (■) and UV + TiO2 photocatalytic treatment (●).

(a) Fluorescent staining with live/dead cells in L929 cell line treated before or after photocatalytic degradation of GQDs (Living cells represented by green, dead cells represented by red); (b) Quantitative analysis of L929 cell viability by MTT assay.

head.ce:title: Toxicity reduction of graphene quantum dots by photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biotechnology and Health Sciences, Wuyi University, Jiangmen 529020, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biotechnology and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread use and uncontrolled diffusion of nano-carbon materials are bringing threats to human health because the cytotoxicity of such materials increases over certain in-vivo concentrations. In the present study, the cytotoxicity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was reduced after photocatalysis with TiO2 and ultraviolet light. The FTIR and TEM analysis suggested that the hydrophilic functional groups of GQDs were removed, causing the aggregation of the nanoparticles and the reduction of water-diffused concentration. The treatment is valuable for the disposal of nano-carbon materials in the wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nano-carbon materials with bright fluorescence, such as graphene (oxide) quantum dots (GQDs), and carbon quantum dots, have attracted considerable attention because their unique fluorescent features have been applied to light devices[1], bio-imaging [2], semiconductors[3], carbon-based catalysts[3] and electrochemistry[4], anti-counterfeiting[5,6] and so forth [7-9]. The widespread use of these nanocarbon materials increases their emission and diffusion to the water of the ecosystem. It brings a potential threat to human health because nanocarbon materials are possibly ingested into the human body through the ecological cycles [10]. These nano carbons will be toxic when their concentration reaches a certain level [11,12]. Therefore, exploring methods to remove or degrade the toxicity of nanocarbon materials is advantageous to reduce the risk of their use in the ecosystem.

As compared to traditional adsorption [13] and physical or chemical separation (membrane) technologies [14], photocatalysis is a green, cost-effective, and sustainable method to remove organic pollutants in water [15-17]. Since the molecular structure of nanocarbon materials is similar to the organics but with larger molecular weight, they may be analogously removed, or at least, some functional groups of them are eliminated. In this way, the toxicity of the nanocarbon-containing water is reduced. In this work, the feasibility of photocatalytic treatment of GQDs to reduce toxicity is demonstrated. Through the analysis of photoluminescence (PL), ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) and in-vitro toxicity test, it was found that the surface functional groups of the GQDs could be sufficiently removed but the backbone could be mostly preserved during the photocatalytic degradation. Thus both the PL intensity and toxicity of GQDs were reduced, leading to the safety requirement of water.

Citrate acid (CA) was employed as the raw material of GQDs. Typically, 2.0 g CA was placed in a glass beaker and then maintained at 180 °C for 4 h [18,19]. The production was dispersed in distilled water with sonication at a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min. The supernatant was collected and stored for further applications.

The surface functional group of the samples was investigated using FTIR（SHIMADZU Ltd., Tokyo, JAPAN)in an IRAffinity-1S type spectrometer. The morphology of samples was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (JEM-F200, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, JAPAN). The fluorescence of the GQDs was measured using a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. The photocatalytic test was carried out by monitoring the luminescent properties of GQDs. Typical, the obtained GQDs solution was diluted to 0.5 mg/mL and 100 mL of the solution was poured into a glass beaker. Then 50 mg of TiO2 (P25) was added and dispersed into the solution. The mixture was first treated using sonication and then stirred for 10 min in dark to reach an adsorption equilibrium. Then an ultraviolet lamp (8 W) with a 365 nm emission wavelength was employed as an excitation source. The status of the GQDs was monitored using the photoluminescence (PL) method, which was realized in a Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer.

Cell viability: The mouse fibroblast cell line NCTC clone 929 (L929) was seeded 1×104 cells/well, and co-incubated with the GQDs (500 μg/mL) before and after photocatalytic degradation. MTT (5 mg/mL in PBS, 50 μL) was added and detected after 24 h incubation. The untreated cells were used as the control group, which cell viability marked as 100%.

Live/dead staining: L929 cells were seeded in a 12-well plate (5×104 cells/well). Before or after photocatalytic degradation, the GQDs (300 μg/mL, 600 μg/mL) were added and co-incubated for 24 h. After that, the culture medium and the extracellular GQDs were removed, and the cells were gently washed twice with a phosphate buffer solution. The Calcein AM stained with living cells and propidium iodide (PI) stained with dead cells were used for fluorescent staining according to the manual (Beyotime, China). Cells were observed by the fluorescence microscope (Calcein AM, Ex/Em = 494/517 nm; PI, Ex/Em = 535/617 nm).

The GQDs were simply prepared by citrate acid as follows (Fig. 1 a). The particle size was defined by an atomic force microscope (AFM) and shown in Fig. 1b. The result showed that the particles were dispersed homogeneously with a maximum size of 1.2 nm. The zeta potential of GQDs at different pH was also measured (Supplemental materials Fig. S1). The result showed that the pHzpc of the GQDs is almost 5, which means that the GQDs may gather at pH = 5 but stay diffusing at other pHs.

The morphology of the GQDs before and after photocatalysis is shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2a, the diameter of the GQDs distributes in 7–10 nm. The crystal plane spacing was found to be about 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (1120) planes of the graphite structure [20]. After photocatalytic treatment, an aggregation phenomenon was observed from the GQDs (Fig. 2b), leading to a larger particle size than the isolated particles. This aggregation indicated the change of surface functional groups and thus the surface energy of the GQDs.

The surface functional groups of the GQDs were characterized using FTIR. As shown in Fig. 3 , the adsorption peaks at about 680 and 1030 cm−1 were respectively ascribed to the bending and stretching vibrational modes of the C = C and C-O-C bonds which constructed the backbone of the GQDs. The peaks at about 1750 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrational mode of C = O bonds. The peaks at about 1380 and 1232 cm−1 were attributed to the O-H bending and C-O stretching vibrational modes, indicating that the functional groups of GQDs were estimated to be the –OH and –COOH groups. A strong absorption band at about 3400 cm−1 was induced by the adsorbed H2O molecules on the surface of the GQDs [21]. After photocatalytic treatment, the absorption peaks of the FTIR corresponding to the –OH and COOH groups nearly vanished. It indicated that most of the surface functional groups were removed during the photocatalytic process. The conjugated surface bonds, such as C = C (680 cm−1 in FTIR) with hydrophobicity were exposed. Thus, the hydrophilic surface was changed to hydrophobic, leading to the disappearance of adsorbed H2O (3400 cm−1 in FTIR) molecules and the appearance of aggregation of the particles.

The excitation and emission spectra of the GQDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. A broad emission band was observed at about 470 nm, which could be effectively excited by 360 and 280 nm excitation bands, corresponding to the π → π* and n → π* transitions [22] −. The n → π* transition is associated with the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs. Thus the change of the functional groups during the photocatalytic process results in the change of the luminescent property of the GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the PL intensity is reduced progressively as the suspended TiO2 particles are excited by the UV lamp, during which the functional groups are removed gradually. As compared, the PL intensity of the control sample without TiO2 suspension was maintained stably in UV. The result indicated that photocatalysis induced by the UV and TiO2 suspension removed the surface functional groups of GQDs (confirmed by FTIR), and then reduced the probability of their n → π* transition. As a result, the PL intensity of GQDs is analogically reduced.

The in vivo toxicity of GQDs was detected by the Zebrafish larvae, which were stimulated by the increased concentration of GQDs and observed under a fluorescence microscope. As shown in Supplemental materials Fig. S2, all the larvae died when the concentration of CQDs was up to 500 µg/mL, and a large area of green fluorescence was presented in the Zebrafish abdomen. Beyond the concentration of 100 µg/mL, the larvae developed normally, and the survival rate was half of the control group. The result demonstrated that the IC50 of GQDs to zebrafish larvae was 100 µg/ml. The in vitro toxicity in the L929 cell line was also detected. GQDs with 500 µg/mL and 250 µg/mL under different conditions were added, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 a, fluorescent staining indicated that almost half of the cells were stained with dead cells (red) in the group of GQDs and GQDs + TiO2, oppositely, GQDs after UV degradation for 1 h significantly reversed the toxicity of the untreated GQDs. For quantitative analysis, we also determined the toxicity of GQDs by MTT assay, as shown in Fig. 5b, the cell viability was reduced to 45% and 49% after being stimulated by the untreated GQDs with 500 µg/mL and 250 µg/mL. in contrast, cell viability remained at 73% and 82% in the group of UV degradation.

The fluorescence of GQDs is induced by the n-π* transition which is associated with the surface functional groups. When photocatalysis is employed to treat the GQDs, the surface functional groups of the GQDs are removed. As a result, the hydrophilic surface of GQDs changes to hydrophobic, causing the reduction of the fluorescence intensity and the aggregation of the particles. The aggregation of them prevents their transportation through the cell wall, thus reducing toxicity. These results demonstrated that photocatalytic degradation may significantly reduce the cell toxicity of GQDs by the increment of aggregation.

In summary, after photocatalytic treatment, the GQDs tend to aggregate due to the loss of hydrophilic groups. Then the probability of n → π* transition is reduced, leading to a decrement in fluorescent intensity. Moreover, the loss of the functional groups renders the aggregation of the particles and then reduces the toxicity of the GQDs since more L929 cells survive in the suspension after the photocatalytic treatment. The method of depriving toxicity of graphene quantum dots by photocatalysis is practical and valuable for the disposal of nano-carbon materials in wastewater.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32000936).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2023.140550.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

J. Mater. Chem. C.
